coredata.dc:title: A signal-amplified ratiometric fluorescence biomimetic sensor based on the synergistic effect of IFE and AE for the visual smart monitoring of oxytetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xuelian
Guo, Ying
Zhang, Jinni
Wang, Xiaohui
Fang, Guozhen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: Exploring high-performance fluorescence strategies for onsite monitoring of oxytetracycline (OTC) is of great significance for protecting human health. Herein, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence biomimetic sensor featured with high sensitivity and selectivity was constructed based on magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots embedded europium metal–organic framework and incorporating molecularly imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP). In this protocol, MIP layer functioning as recognition units endowed Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP with satisfactory binding efficiency and anti-interference ability. Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs as both supporter and signal tag enabled the sensor to not only demonstrate a fast fluorescence response, but also yield reverse response signals depending on the synergistic effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and antenna effect (AE), achieving signal amplification. Benefiting from the efficient dual-channel strategy, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP exhibited a wide linear range (0.02–50 μg mL−1) with a sensitive detection limit (6.6 ng mL−1) and a marked color transformation (blue-to-red), which was highly desirable for monitoring of oxytetracycline. Simultaneously, aiming at attaining precise onsite analysis, a portable intelligent sensing platform was devised by integrating Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP and a smartphone-assisted device. The smartphone with an installed color recognizer application as signal reader and analyzer realized oxytetracycline visual sensing by capturing and digitizing fluorescence images. More significantly, the portable platform was successfully applied to the onsite monitoring of OTC in water samples and milk samples with acceptable results. Accordingly, the as-developed platform demonstrated low-cost, portability, easy implementation, desirable specificity and sensitivity, presenting great potential for point-of-care testing.

head.ce:title: A signal-amplified ratiometric fluorescence biomimetic sensor based on the synergistic effect of IFE and AE for the visual smart monitoring of oxytetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP and the process of ratiometric fluorescence sensing and smartphone-based visual assay of trace oxytetracycline in real samples.

Exploring high-performance fluorescence strategies for onsite monitoring of oxytetracycline (OTC) is of great significance for protecting human health. Herein, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence biomimetic sensor featured with high sensitivity and selectivity was constructed based on magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots embedded europium metal–organic framework and incorporating molecularly imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP). In this protocol, MIP layer functioning as recognition units endowed Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP with satisfactory binding efficiency and anti-interference ability. Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs as both supporter and signal tag enabled the sensor to not only demonstrate a fast fluorescence response, but also yield reverse response signals depending on the synergistic effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and antenna effect (AE), achieving signal amplification. Benefiting from the efficient dual-channel strategy, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP exhibited a wide linear range (0.02–50 μg mL−1) with a sensitive detection limit (6.6 ng mL−1) and a marked color transformation (blue-to-red), which was highly desirable for monitoring of oxytetracycline. Simultaneously, aiming at attaining precise onsite analysis, a portable intelligent sensing platform was devised by integrating Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP and a smartphone-assisted device. The smartphone with an installed color recognizer application as signal reader and analyzer realized oxytetracycline visual sensing by capturing and digitizing fluorescence images. More significantly, the portable platform was successfully applied to the onsite monitoring of OTC in water samples and milk samples with acceptable results. Accordingly, the as-developed platform demonstrated low-cost, portability, easy implementation, desirable specificity and sensitivity, presenting great potential for point-of-care testing.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC), a significant and common antibiotic, has been widely employed for the prevention and treatment of diseases resulted from bacterial infections because of the broad-spectrum activity, cheap price and powerful therapeutic effect [1–3]. Unfortunately, the overuse of OTC as feed additive during poultry rearing leads to drug accumulation in foodstuffs and environmental media, which poses a serious threat to public health through food chain transmission, including allergic reactions, liver damage, and bacterial resistance to antibiotics [4]. To safeguard consumer health, many countries have established the maximum residue limits (MRLs) of TCs remaining in milk, including China (100 µg kg−1), the European Union (225 nM) [2,5]. As a result, it is highly desirable to develop a simple and effective stratagem to rapidly and precisely monitor trace OTC concentration for early warning.

Multiple analysis strategies, involving high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) [7], enzyme-linked immunoassay [8], electrochemical analysis [9], capillary electrophoresis [10], colorimetry [11] and fluorometry [12], have been exploited and utilized for the detection of OTC. Compared with other technologies, fluorescence analysis holds the merits of simplicity, low-cost, ultra-fast responses, high sensitivity and easy implementation of visualization monitoring capabilities, which enables it to be a more effective nondestructive online monitoring approach [13]. In the past few years, researchers have attempted to develop some fluorescent sensing platforms for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) [14]. However, most of the reported fluorescence sensors rely on a single channel fluorescence response, which is prone to be interfered by instrument, environment and probe concentration during the sensing process [15]. Encouragingly, ratiometric FL sensors with double channels can provide a built-in self-calibration for signal, which is beneficial to gain a more sensitive and reliable analysis results in practical applications [16,17]. At present, some lanthanide-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors, especially europium ions (Eu3+)-based fluorescence sensing platform have been employed for the detection of TCs, since TCs contain a β-diketonate configuration that as a ligand can chelate Eu3+ and simultaneously sensitize Eu3+ through “antenna effect” [2,16]. Unfortunately, these sensors often suffer from poor stability and unnoticeable FL color transformation, restricting their further application to a large extent [12,18]. Accordingly, preparing novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor with high sensitivity and favorable stability will be a promising aspect for fulfilling the visualization analysis of OTC.

Luminescence metal–organic frameworks (LMOFs) belonging to newly emerged fascinating porous crystalline materials, have been proverbially applied to the construction of fluorescence sensors owing to the virtues of ultrahigh surface area, excellent chemical stability, abundant functional groups and tunable emission properties [19]. Notably, lanthanide MOFs (Ln-MOFs) with prominent luminescent properties (i.e. long fluorescence lifetimes and large Stokes shift value) exhibit great prospects applications in chemo- and bio-sensing [20]. With the advent of nanotechnology, nanoparticles with easy-to-modify size and shape present highly sophisticated specifications that allow them to be a part of any novel and promising materials [21,22]. More recently, a few simple yet valid novel ratiometric sensing platforms based on the encapsulation of the organic dyes [23], perovskite nanocrystals [24], silicon nanoparticles [25] or carbon dots [26] into Ln-MOFs have been established for monitoring antibiotics. These developed ratiometric fluorescence strategies achieved signal amplification owing to the doping of fluorescent nanoparticles and the large surface area and high porosity of LMOF. More significantly, the as-prepared multifunctional Ln-MOF-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors not only maintain the stable crystal structure of Ln-MOFs, but also integrate the luminescence characteristics of nanoparticles and Ln-MOFs, which enables the sensors to display superior properties to the individual components [27]. Besides, among the aforementioned composites, the encapsulation of CDs can endow the hybrid sensor with more prominent advantages compared with other nanoparticles (organic dyes, silicon nanoparticles), including fascinating optical properties, physicochemical stability, biocompatibility and low-toxicity [28]. Thus, the ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on the encapsulation of CDs into Ln-MOFs is more favorable as a promising means to fabricate excellent ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Nevertheless, the common sensors still have certain limitation such as weak anti-interference ability, causing the sensing system to be easily affected by coexisting substances in the process of detecting the target antibiotic. Therefore, the development of LMOF-based ratiometric sensor with extraordinary selectivity will be an exceedingly important means to promote precise monitoring of antibiotics.

Molecular imprinting technique (MIT), an efficient and versatile tool capable of enhancing the selective recognition ability for target molecules, has been hotly utilized to synthesize molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [29,30]. As effective alternatives to plastic antibodies, the synthesized MIPs have emerged in the fields of extraction/separation [31], catalysis [32], drug delivery [33], and sensing [34] because of the appealing merits of strong affinity, superior thermal and chemical stability, desired specificity and selectivity, as well as good reusability [35]. However, conventional MIPs prepared by bulk imprinting are faced with the problem of non-uniform distribution of binding sites, deep embedded cavities, long analysis time and relatively limited selectivity because of the need for grinding and sieving in the preparation process and the lack of support materials [35,36]. Fortunately, sol–gel strategy, as a green synthetic method, has been used to prepare MIPs due to the virtues of the simplicity of preparation and mild polymerization conditions. Meanwhile, as both supporting materials and signal transducer, LMOFs with large surface area and high porosity have been introduced to fabricate the core–shell molecularly imprinted composites [37]. For example, MIP/Zr-LMOF, a nanostructured MIPs based on luminescent zirconium metal–organic framework (Zr-LMOF) as the supporter, was constructed and used for the rapid sensing of chloramphenicol [38]. Lately, in light of the sol–gel strategy, our research group designed and synthesized a novel core–shell molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor (UCNPs@ZIF-8@MIP) for ultrasensitive detection and highly selective recognition of octopamine [39]. Compared with traditional MIPs, the MIP/LMOFs composites with the special structure of core–shell exhibited faster mass transfer and higher sensitivity owing to presence of the supporter [30]. However, it is worth noting that the previously developed MIP/LMOFs-based sensors still suffer from low portability in practical applications, which is not conducive to high-speed and onsite detection of OTC. Thence, exploring novel strategies for the construction of high-quality portable device based on MIP/LMOFs is of great significance for point-of-care monitoring of OTC.

Smartphones, as high-performance detectors containing all of the components required for a common analytical laboratory device, have received wide attention in point-of-care (POC) sensing due to their simple operation, small size, fast processing speed, and high resolution [40]. As the promising sample-to-answer analyzers, smartphones can transduce signals (via camera), perform analysis with installing applications (apps), and display results on the screen, which provided a low-cost and portable monitoring platform for onsite detection [41,42]. Currently, a series of FL strategies based on the smartphone have been designed and applied for the determination of various target species [43]. In particular, the smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent sensing platforms are more conducive to achieving point-of-care monitoring in accurate quantitation due to the intrinsic correction function. For instance, Ye and co-workers reported a smartphone-based QDs ratiometric fluorescence strategy to achieve the onsite quantitation analysis of fluoroquinolone antibiotics in a series of actual water and food samples [44]. Li et al. employed silicon oxide-coated CuNCs (CuNCs@SiO2) as a signal reference and cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs) as a signal response to fabricate a novel smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, which was successfully applied to the point-of-care test of cadmium ions [45]. However, to our knowledge, a portable intelligent ratiometric FL detection platform for the onsite sensing OTC based on Mg,N-CDs-functionalized europium metal–organic frameworks imprinted polymer as the signal tag has not been reported yet.

Enlightened by the aforementioned facts, a facile yet efficient dual-channel ratiometric fluorescent strategy was designed for assay of OTC based on Mg,N-CDs-doped europium metal–organic frameworks coupled with molecularly imprinted polymer. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , Mg,N-CDs with extremely bright blue fluorescence were first prepared via one-pot hydrothermal approach. Next, the dual-emission Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs were constructed for the first time through a facile self-assembly strategy based on the encapsulation of ultra-small Mg,N-CDs into Eu-MOFs. Afterward, the imprinting layer was fabricated by the sol–gel technology based on Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs as both fluorophore and supporting matrix, OTC as imprinted template, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) as functional monomer, and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as cross-linking agent. After template elution, core–shell Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP with 3D cavities was finally acquired. In the presence of OTC, the blue fluorescent at 445 nm (Mg,N-CDs) was validly quenched thanks to IFE, while red fluorescence at 620 nm (Eu3+) was significantly increased because of the energy transfer defined as “antenna effect” (AE). Herein, the efficient dual-channel strategy based on the synergistic effect of IFE and AE enabled Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor to demonstrate double and reverse response signals accompanied by a distinguished blue-to-red fluorescent color evolution, achieving ultrasensitivity and visual detection of OTC. More importantly, with the decoration of the imprinting layer, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibited superior binding efficiency and satisfactory specificity for OTC. Additionally, taking account of the requirements of onsite quantification in practical applications, a portable smartphone-integrated optical device by combining the proposed dual-emission Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was further developed, which provided a novel strategy for the point-of-care testing (POCT) of OTC in real samples. The practical application of developed ratiometric fluorescence sensor and smartphone-based point-of-care testing platform was finally confirmed by monitoring the water samples and milk samples.

Europium chloride (EuCl3) was purchased from TCI Chemical Reagent Factory (Shanghai, China). 5-boronobenzene-1,3-dicarboxylic acid (BBDC), TEOS, APTES, OTC, doxycycline (DOX), tetracycline (TC), kanamycin sulfate (KAN) and chloroamphenicol (CAP) were provided by Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chlortetracycline hydrochloride was provided from Tan-Mo Technology Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). The optical properties of materials, including UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra, were analyzed using a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a Hitachi F-7100 (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns at diffraction angles from 5° to 50° were recorded on a D8advance X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The morphology and structure were observed by JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Japan) and S4800 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Japan).
Mg,N-CDs were synthesized according to our previously reported approach [46]. The detailed preparation process was described in Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Mg,N-CDs-doped europium metal–organic frameworks
ce:section.ce:para: Referring to the literature method [47], Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs were prepared via the following improved processes. Typically, as-synthesized Mg,N-CDs (10 mg), EuCl3 (0.1 mmol) and BBDC (0.1 mmol) were mixed with 3 mL of water and 7 mL of DMF under vigorously stirring at room temperature for 2 h. After that, the mixture was moved to an autoclave and heated to 130 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the obtained yellowish solution was purified through centrifuging and washing with DMF and ethanol to remove the unreacted substances. Finally, the pale-yellow Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs powder was obtained through drying overnight. Besides, Eu-MOFs were synthesized according to the before-mentioned procedure, except that no Mg,N-CDs were added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Mg,N-CDs-doped europium metal–organic frameworks imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para: Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was synthesized according to a reported sol–gel process with some modification [48,49]. First, the as-prepared Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs particles (30 mg) and OTC (0.1 mmol) were dispersed uniformly in 9 mL of ethanol under sonication. APTES (0.6 mmol) was then added dropwise to the above reaction system and kept constantly magnetic stirring for 1 h at room temperature to form a pre-polymerization mixture. Next, TEOS (2 mmol) and HOAc (1 mL, 0.1 mM) were sequentially dropped into the reaction system and stirred for 6 h. OTC imprinted composite was collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol to remove free reactants. Then, the composite was further eluted with ethanol/water (v/v, 1:1) until no OTC was detected with UV–vis spectrophotometer. Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was finally acquired via drying overnight. Meanwhile, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP (NIP), non-imprinted polymer, was prepared in a manner identical to Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP (MIP) in the absence of OTC. Compared to MIP, NIP without imprinting sites adsorbed a few target molecules. Therefore, the prepared Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP as a contrast material was used to highlight the importance of tailor-made imprinting sites of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP in this work. By adopting Eu-MOFs as the supporter, Eu-MOFs@MIP as a contrast material was synthesized in the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for ratiometric FL determination of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescence detection procedure for OTC was carried out as follows. 0.25 mg mL−1 Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP solution was mixed with different concentrations OTC standard solution (v/v, 1:1) at ambient temperature in a tube. After incubating for 2 min, the fluorescence spectra were obtained with an excitation at 370 nm. The selectivity was assessed by introducing other antibiotics or potential interfering species to replace OTC in accordance with the above approach. In order to ensure the reliability of the experimental results, three repeated measurements were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the portable device for onsite assay
ce:section.ce:para: Given the interference of sunlight and environment during the detection, a facile detection device was designed and developed through the integration of smartphone, optical detection module and light-proof cassette. The synoptic diagram of the homemade device was demonstrated in Fig. 6B, and the important units were presented in Fig. S1. Herein, a 365 nm UV lamp initiated by a portable power bank was fixed on a lamp holder and served as the excitation light source to excite the as-prepared Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP composite. A sample cell with a groove for holding cuvette was equipped in the device, which was applied to fixing the distance between sample and light source. Meanwhile, a smartphone with an installed color recognizer application and a camera was employed as the signal reader and analyzer to convert the fluorescent color images into digital values related to the red (R), green (G), and blue (B) color channels. And a filter (400 nm) was set between the sample cell and the smartphone to eliminate the interference from excitation luminous beam. Furthermore, the cassette with a cavity that can collect photos via a smartphone was composed of black steel plate material and used as a supporter to integrate all of the above parts for executing the onsite monitoring.

The lake water obtained from Beitang lake (Tianjin, China) and milk purchased from the local supermarket were taken as real samples to evaluate the practical application of the proposed detection methods. The water samples were first centrifuged and filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane to remove insoluble impurities. Then the appropriate concentration of OTC was spiked in the samples for further quantitative analysis [50]. The pretreatment of milk samples was implemented following a reported procedure with slight modifications [51]. Briefly, 1 mL milk samples containing different concentrations of OTC (1 μg mL−1, 5 μg mL−1, 25 μg mL−1) were placed into a 15 mL centrifuge tube and mixed with 4 mL acetonitrile. After vortexing for 5 min, the mixture was carried out by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) and filtration with a 0.22 μm filter to remove the precipitation of protein. Subsequently, the samples were blown dry with nitrogen and redispersed in 1 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7, 50 mM). OTC spiked samples were finally analyzed according to the proposed ratiometric fluorescence determination method and smartphone-based point-of-care testing platform.
For HPLC analysis, the pretreatment process of milk and sample analysis procedures were devised and executed in conformity to the Chinese National Standard (GB/T 22990–2008).

The morphologies of Mg,N-CDs, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, NIP (Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP) and MIP (Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP) were investigated by SEM and TEM images. As presented in Fig. S2A, the resulting Mg,N-CDs displayed good dispersibility and a relatively uniform morphology with an average diameter of about 4 nm, which was consistent with the reported literature [46,52]. Meanwhile, the corresponding energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrum (Fig. S2B) and the elemental mapping image (Fig. S2C) demonstrated the existence of characteristic elements, including C, N, O and Mg, which further verified the successful synthesis of Mg,N-CDs. Interestingly, Mg,N-CDs demonstrated a high fluorescence quantum yield (32.6 %) because of the synergistic effect between nitrogen and magnesium. As revealed in Fig. 1 , the as-synthesized Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs exhibited a rough spherical morphology with a porous structure and the particle size of approximately 3.65 μm (Fig. S3). The mapping image (Fig. S4) illustrated that both magnesium and nitrogen were present and homogeneously distributed in the composite material, which confirmed that Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs were triumphantly prepared by encapsulating Mg,N-CDs into Eu-MOF. Compared with Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, MIP and NIP showed a distinct shell-core structure (Fig. 1 E and F), and the particle diameter was increased to about 4.55 μm (Fig. S5), which initially manifested that the formation of polymers. Additionally, the EDS spectrum (Fig. S6) and elemental mapping image (Fig. S7) of MIP further enunciated that the imprinting shell has been decorated on the surface of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs.

To ascertain the functional groups of the composite materials prepared in each step, FT-IR spectroscopy was implemented and recorded. As displayed in Fig. 1G, a distinct absorption peak (1370 cm−1) correspond to the vibration of B-O derived from Eu-MOF [53,54] and the characteristic broad peaks of N–H (1656 and 3320 cm−1) and O–H (3320 cm−1) correlated with Mg,N-CDs all emerged in Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs curve, implying that Mg,N-CDs have been embedded into Eu-MOFs [16,55]. For MIP and NIP curves, the absorption peak located at 2963 cm−1 was attributed to the antisymmetric stretching vibration of C–H bond on silane [16], and the absorption bands at 1056, 794 and 459 cm−1 corresponded to Si-O-Si and Si-O groups [3,56], which meant the presence of APTES and TEOS. It is worth noting that the characteristic peaks of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs became inconspicuous in MIP and NIP spectra, indicating that imprinting layer was successfully wrapped on the surface of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs. Additionally, there was no significant difference between MIP and NIP spectra in the position of characteristic peaks. The above results validated the successful construction of the sensor (Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP). The powder XRD patterns of Eu-MOFs, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, MIP and NIP were investigated and portrayed in Fig. 1H. Apparently, the diffraction peaks of bare Eu-MOFs were agreed well with that of the simulated data of the literature [47,54]. After embedding the Mg,N-CDs into Eu-MOFs, there was a negligible change in the powder XRD pattern, which illustrated that Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs maintaining the crystal structure of Eu-MOFs were synthesized. However, of particular note was that the characteristic diffraction peaks of Eu-MOFs basically disappeared in MIP and NIP curves thanks to the adornment of the imprinting shell, which further manifested polymers formation [56]. Meanwhile, the porosity of Eu-MOFs, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, MIP and NIP was clarified by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig. 1I). The corresponding Brunauer-Emmett-Teller surface areas of Eu-MOFs, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, MIP and NIP were 49 m2 g−1, 32 m2 g−1, 14 m2 g−1 and 11 m2 g−1, respectively. Obviously, compared with Eu-MOFs, the surface area of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs remarkably decreased, which indicated that Mg,N-CDs were triumphantly embedded into Eu-MOFs to form the hybrid material Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs [28]. Furthermore, MIP and NIP exhibited a slightly lower surface area than Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, which was ascribed to the modification of imprinting layer [20,56]. Taken together, the above results revealed a core–shell sensor was triumphantly prepared through Mg,N-CDs-doped europium metal–organic frameworks coupled with molecularly imprinted polymer.

The optical performance of the resulting sensor was discussed through FL spectra. As presented in Fig. 2 A, FL spectrum of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs coincided with that of Mg,N-CDs, revealing that the process of encapsulating Mg,N-CD into Eu-MOFs scarcely interfered the luminescence performance of Mg,N-CD. Compared with Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, FL intensity of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP at 445 nm slightly reduced owing to the decoration of imprinting shell. However, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP presented the emission peak (445 nm) corresponding to Mg,N-CDs, indicating a negligible influence of the encapsulation of imprinting layer and the introduction of Eu-MOFs on the photoluminescence process of Mg,N-CDs. These results implied that Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP maintained unique FL performance of Mg,N-CDs. After adding OTC to Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP sensing system, in addition to the emission peak of Mg,N-CDs, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP simultaneously emitted the characteristic peaks (590–710 nm) stemmed from Eu3+ transition (5D0 → 7FJ, J = 1–4), which demonstrated that Eu3+ ions were successfully sensitized by the “antenna” molecules OTC. Meanwhile, since the emission peak at 620 nm exhibited the most distinct response to OTC, it was considered to be the characteristic output signal of Eu3+. Together these measurements clarified that a double-signal response sensor integrating optical properties of Mg,N-CDs and Eu-MOFs was successfully constructed. And the fluorescence signal ratio (I 620/I 445) as the parameter can be utilized for ratiometric sensing of OTC. Furthermore, in order to determine the appropriate excitation wavelength of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-OTC sensing system, the excitation and emission spectra of the system were investigated. Fig. S8 illustrated that there were two excitation peaks around 350 and 370 nm, and the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra at the different excitation wavelengths also were obtained. Taking into account the practical application, the excitation wavelength was assessed using I 620/I 445 value as a measurement index. Fig. S8 revealed that the optimal excitation wavelength of the sensing system was 370 nm, and it was employed for the subsequent research.

Aiming at estimating the photostability of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor, FL response was measured under different conditions, including UV irradiation time and storage time. As depicted in Fig. S9A, I620/I 445 was almost not influenced by UV irradiation time, which meant that the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor possessed desirable anti-photobleaching property. Meanwhile, it can be seen from Fig. S9B that the FL signal (I 620/I 445) of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP remained basically unchanged within 120 days, revealing that the sensor enjoyed superior storage stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and sensing mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the developed dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for assay of OTC was first explored by introducing OTC to sensing system. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, for the as-prepared polymers binding to the target molecules, FL intensity of Mg,N-CDs at 445 nm was quenched, while the emission intensity of Eu3+ at 620 nm was enhanced remarkably. By contrast, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP appeared more prominent FL change than Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP in the existence of OTC, which manifested that Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP had a stronger affinity to target molecule. However, when the imprinted OTC was removed, FL intensity of polymers was restored. Interestingly, it was found that FL spectrum of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was highly in accordance with that of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP, denoting that the proposed dual-channel strategy based on Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was feasible for ratiometric determination of OTC.

Next, the possible mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence sensor for OTC was systematically studied and validated. At first, to clarify the fluorescence quenching principle of Mg,N-CDs caused by OTC, UV–vis absorption spectrum of OTC and FL spectrum of Mg,N-CDs were provided (Fig. 2C). Noticeably, the excitation spectrum of Mg,N-CDs partially overlapped with UV–vis absorption spectrum of OTC, which initially manifested the possibility of IFE. Concretely speaking, fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore is invariable during IFE, while fluorescence lifetime reduces for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [15,57,58]. Accordingly, the fluorescence lifetime as an effective indicator was investigated to further enunciate the quenching mechanism. As portrayed in Fig. S10, the fluorescence lifetime of Mg,N-CDs displayed a negligible change after adding OTC to the sensing system, which further certified that OTC-induced FL quenching behavior was predominantly due to IFE instead of FRET. Simultaneously, the target-triggered FL enhancing principle of Eu3+ was discussed in-depth. As depicted in Fig. 2D, after being coordinated with OTC, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP presented the absorption peak of OTC. However, it is worth noting that the UV–vis absorption peak of the sensing system containing Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP and OTC displayed a significant red shift, and the absorption peak intensity of sensing system remarkably increased compared with that of OTC, which revealed that OTC containing β-diketonate configuration chelated with Eu3+ of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP to form a Eu3+-OTC complex. The above phenomenon was attributed to the fact that OTC possesses the higher coordination strength to Eu3+ than water molecules [16,18]. Thence, the introduced OTC can replace water molecules and chelate with Eu3+, and transfer the absorbed energy to Eu3+ through “antenna effect”, thereby greatly enhancing the red luminescence at 620 nm of the sensor [1,2,59]. Additionally, fluorescence lifetime further confirmed that there was an efficient energy transfer between Eu3+ and OTC, because the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-MOFs in Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP prolonged from 78 μs to 349 μs (Fig. S11) with the increase of OTC concentration. In this dual-channel strategy, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs acted as the signal source can achieve two reverse changes in fluorescence signals, in which the FL enhancement was related to AE, and the FL quenching was associated with IFE. Therefore, the developed sensor can present a distinguished blue-to-red fluorescent color change with the addition of OTC, which provided a prerequisite for visual assay of OTC.

To obtain the more excellent performance for ratiometric determination of OTC, the relevant sensing conditions were optimized, including reaction time and pH of the solution. First, the kinetics of polymers towards OTC were explored by monitoring the changes in FL signal (I 620/I 445) of the detection system at different response times. As shown in Fig. 2E, with the increase of response time, I 620/I 445 value of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP increased first and then tended to be constant, indicating that the sensing system reached equilibrium at 2 min. The rapid FL response of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP towards OTC benefited from the large surface area and porosity of Eu-MOFs that committed to improving the binding efficiency of target molecules and recognition sites. Thus, 2 min was taken as the optimal reaction time for follow-up tests. It was noteworthy that the fluorescence response trend of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP was highly in accordance with that of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP. However, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP demonstrated a faster adsorption rate and superior binding capacity than Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP at the identical incubation time, which was attributed to the introduction of OTC-imprinted sites. Subsequently, pH value of the reaction system was also optimized by analyzing fluorescence response of the sensor to different solution pH in the presence and absence of OTC. As displayed in Fig. 2F, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was insensitive to pH without the presence of OTC, which meant that the sensor possessed a good stability in the pH range of 5–9. However, in the presence of OTC, the maximum FL response signal occurred at pH 7, revealing that binding efficiency of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP towards OTC reached the highest. Therefore, pH 7 was applied for subsequent analysis of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of ratiometric fluorescence detection method for OTC
ce:section.ce:para: Under the before-mentioned optimal conditions, concentration-dependent photoluminescent property of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence approach was investigated by analyzing FL variation of the sensor to different levels of OTC. As the OTC concentration increased (Fig. 3 A), the characteristic emission peak of Eu3+ at 620 nm progressively increased, whereas the emission peak of Mg,N-CDs (445 nm) continuously declined under the excitation of 370 nm. Evidently, Fig. 3C illustrated that a satisfactory linear relationship between FL response (I 620/I 445) of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP and OTC concentration was achieved in the range of 0.02 to 50 μg mL−1. And the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 6.6 ng mL−1 (14.3 nM) according to the standard 3σ/slope [2,20], which was much lower than MRLs of OTC stipulated by the European Union (225 nM) and China (100 μg/kg) [15,41]. The analyte-dependent FL spectra of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP were recorded in Fig. S12A. By contrast, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP displayed a more remarkable FL change than Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP with the increase same amount of OTC, which denoted the exceptional recognition capability of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP towards OTC. Meanwhile, the high selectivity of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP to OTC was further confirmed by comparing the slopes of linear equations (Fig. 3C) of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP (I 620/I 445 = 0.1415C OTC + 0.0361) and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP (I 620/I 445 = 0.0287C OTC + 0.0346). Of particular note was that the constructed Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP presented a more marked blue-to-red color change (Fig. 3D) than Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP (Fig. S12B) with the increase of OTC concentration. Additionally, the color evolution corresponding to Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was easily recognized by the bare eyes under a 365 nm UV lamp, and it was also in accordance with the CIE chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 3B). The above findings indicated that the remarkable color transformation of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP provided a prerequisite for the real-time monitoring of OTC, and the sensitivity of developed ratiometric fluorescence detection method completely met the requirement for safety control and food quality.

For comparison, Eu-MOFs@MIP served as a single-signal fluorescence sensor was utilized for the quantitative analysis of OTC under the same detection procedures. As delineated in Fig. 4 A and C, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs@MIP at 620 nm exhibited a good linear response to OTC in the range of 0.1–30 μg mL−1 with a limit of detection of 32.9 ng mL−1. Apparently, the dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP displayed a wider linear range and a lower LOD compared to the single-signal fluorescence sensor, which may stem from the built-in self-calibration function of ratiometric fluorescence sensor [1,16]. More importantly, with the addition of OTC, ratiometric fluorescence sensing system exhibited an obvious color variation, while the single-channel detection system only showed a brightness change for the different levels of OTC (Fig. 4B and D). The above results strongly demonstrated that ratiometric fluorescent sensor (Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP) possessed more prominent advantages than the single emission sensor (Eu-MOFs@MIP), such as a wider linear range, higher sensitivity and accuracy, remarkable color evolution. Thus, the resulting Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based sensing system was more suitable for the visual assay of OTC.

The selectivity of the established sensing system was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence response caused by OTC and other antibiotics with similar structural (TC, CTC, DOX, CAP and KAN). Aiming at understanding the specific binding performance of the as-prepared sensor to the target molecule, Fig. S13 presented the structures of these antibiotics. For Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs (Fig. 5 A), the FL response (I 620/I 445) generated by antibiotics (OTC, TC, CTC and DOX) was notably higher than other analytes, which was ascribed to the fact that tetracycline antibiotics contain β-diketonate configuration that as a ligand can chelate Eu3+ and simultaneously sensitize Eu3+ through “antenna effect” [2,16]. Thus, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs exhibited a stronger fluorescence selectivity for tetracycline antibiotics than other analogs. Fortunately, as an effective means, imprinting technology can avoid the interference of potential antibiotics on OTC fluorescence determination. As can be seen, only OTC triggered a remarkable fluorescence change to Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP, while other antibiotics yielded a negligible effect on FL response of the sensing system. The phenomenon was probably ascribed to the noncomplementarity between analogs and OTC-imprinted sites in size, shape and functional groups. The OTC-imprinted cavities formed by the hydrogen-bonding interactions between the template molecule (OTC) and the functional monomer APTES (amino group) displayed much stronger affinity and preferential selectivity for OTC during adsorption. However, there were no recognition sites that completely matched the size, shape and functional group of antibiotics (OTC, TC, CTC and DOX) in Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP, which made it difficult to capture these analogs. In addition, compared with Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@NIP exhibited a lower I 620/I 445 value in the presence of analytes (OTC, TC, CTC, DOX, CAP and KAN) due to the lack of specific recognition sites. The aforementioned results implied that the introduction of imprinting layer with tailor-made sites can enable Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP to accurately capture and efficiently combine with OTC.

To further explore the specific recognition ability of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based sensing system for OTC in complex environments, the competitive experiment was executed by choosing TC, CTC, DOX, CAP and KAN as the potential coexisting antibiotics. As displayed in Fig. 5B, the resulting Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP maintained good recognition ability and specificity for OTC in the coexistence system of target molecules and analogs (TC, CTC, DOX, CAP and KAN), even if the analogs were added at a high concentration (2-fold that of OTC). The distinguished specificity of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP towards OTC can be ascribed to the fluorescence selectivity of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs. On the other hand, there were OTC-imprinted sites formed by hydrogen bonding interaction on the surface of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP, which contributed to selective binding of target molecules and inhibition non-specific adsorption of structural analogs. As a consequence, the double screening function based on imprinted layer and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs enabled the proposed Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP to display favorable specificity towards OTC.

Simultaneously, considering the complex compositions of real samples, the desirable anti-interference ability was very necessary for the developed innovative sensor. Thus, the FL response of the sensor for various possible interfering analytes was investigated in the absence and presence of OTC, including amino acids (Tyr, L-Cys, L-His, L-Phe, L-Ser, L-Arg), saccharides (sucrose and glucose), and common metal ions (Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl−, NO3 –). As portrayed in Fig. 5C, the influences of these potential interfering species on the I 620/I 445 value were similar to that of blank and significantly lower than that of OTC. However, after the addition of OTC, I 620/I 445 value of the sensing system was enhanced, which revealed that the coexistence of interfering species scarcely interfered the fluorescence sensing of OTC. More importantly, compared with other analytes, only OTC can induce a significant fluorescent color variation under the identical conditions (Fig. 5D), which can be easily observed by the naked eye with the aid of commonly used UV lamp (365 nm). These results illustrated that Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based sensing approach had excellent selectivity toward OTC and provided a promising tool for onsite qualitative identification of OTC.

Reusability, as an important performance in the real application, was estimated by repeatedly measuring FL response of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based sensor during adsorption–desorption cycles. As presented in Fig. S14A, FL response of the sensor exhibited a slight change that was 4.4% of its initial value after five sequential cycles of adsorption-regeneration, underlining that the sensor enjoyed a good sensing performance in the monitoring of OTC. Meanwhile, TEM image (Fig. S14B) further confirmed that the core–shell structure of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was maintained unchanged after five cycles. The above findings demonstrated that the resulting sensor possessed favorable reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of OTC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate feasibility of the developed sensing approach in real samples, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was applied to monitoring OTC in water samples and milk samples. However, no OTC was detected during the detection of blank samples using the proposed FL approach, which was consistent with the result of the HPLC method implemented in accordance with the Chinese National Standard (GB/T 22990–2008). Subsequently, the spike and recovery experiments were implemented by adding the different concentrations of OTC standard solutions to samples. The related results were calculated and summarized in Table 1 . A satisfactory recovery rate (90.58% − 96.79%) and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of<3.3 % were obtained using Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based fluorescence method, which was comparable to the HPLC approach. These results implied that the constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensing system based on Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was highly applicable for the quantitative analysis of OTC in real samples. Besides, the sensing performance of the established fluorescence approach was evaluated by comparing with the previously developed fluorescence-based sensing approaches for the detection of OTC. As depicted in Table 2 , the as-constructed fluorescence method in this study demonstrated a wider linear range, a lower detection limit, a faster fluorescence response, superior reusability, good precision (RSD) and comparable recovery.

The superiority of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was attributed to three aspects: (i) As both the supporter and fluorophores, dual-emitting Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs with large surface area and high porosity can not only improve the mass transfer rate and binding capacity of the sensing system, achieving a rapid fluorescence response to OTC, but also yield a reverse variation in two fluorescence signals depending on the synergistic effect of AE and IFE, realizing signal amplification and subsequently enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of ratiometric fluorescent sensor; (ii) OTC-imprinted sites formed by molecular imprinting strategy enabled the sensor to more efficient capture target molecules and avoid unnecessary pre-processing in real sample analysis, which can boost the selectivity of sensing system and reduce the sample analysis time; (iii) Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP composite was easily recycled from the detection system by centrifugal separation owing to hydrogen bond interaction between Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP and OTC, which endowed the sensor with a good reusability. Taken together, the proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor offered an efficient and effective method for sensitive evaluating OTC in complex real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted portable device as sensing platform for real-time monitoring of OTC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Though the above Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor possessed excellent sensitivity for OTC detection, it was not suitable for onsite analysis due to the requirement of expensive and bulky fluorophotometer. In addition, the visual analysis based on the naked eye was not conducive to distinguishing slight color changes. Thence, in order to satisfy precise onsite monitoring of OTC, we devised and developed a portable intelligent sensing platform by the integration of homemade smartphone-assisted device and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 B, the portable homemade device of sensing platform was composed of smartphone, light source (365 nm UV lamp), sample slot, optical filter and light-proof cassette. The device not only provided a dark working environment and a light source with the specific excitation wavelength, but also determined the distance from the samples to light source and smartphone, thereby improving the feasibility and accuracy of detection. More importantly, as the signal reader and analyzer, the smartphone with an installed color recognizer application (Color Picker APP) can realize real-time monitoring of OTC by converting the fluorescence color change into the corresponding analyte concentration. Fig. 6A depicted the analysis process of smartphone-integrated sensing platform. First, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was exposed to a series of solutions containing different OTC concentrations. After incubating for 2 min, the above mixed solution was transferred into a clean cuvette and placed in the sample slot of homemade device. Subsequently, the smartphone was applied to capturing the concentration-dependent fluorescence images under a 365 nm UV lamp. Meanwhile, color images were digitized and transformed into RGB values using Color Picker APP downloaded onto the smartphone. Since the photographs of sensing platform presented a continuous color change from blue to red as the increasement of OTC concentration, the ratio of red (R) and blue (B) values as a parameter can be utilized for detection of OTC. As illustrated in Fig. 6C, there was a favorable linear relationship between the value of R/B and the concentration of OTC in the range of 0.08–50 μg mL−1, and the corresponding linear equation can be expressed by R/B = 0.0292C OTC + 0.2418 (R 2 = 0.993). Besides, a low detection limit of 25.8 ng mL−1 was obtained via 3σ (standard deviation). The above results indicated that the proposed portable smartphone-assisted platform had a great application potential for quantitative assay of OTC.

Similarly, commercial milk and lake water were served as real samples to assess practicability of smartphone-based detection platform. As demonstrated in Table 3 , the recoveries of the smartphone-based sensing method were in the range of 87.02 % − 93.57 %, and the corresponding relative standard deviation (RSD) was <4.4 %. Evidently, the satisfactory results of smartphone-based sensing strategy were consistent with that of HPLC approach and that of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP-based ratiometric fluorescence method, which implied that the proposed method possessed good reliability for detecting OTC in real samples. Therefore, the smartphone-based sensing platform made the assay suitable for OTC onsite monitoring even in resource-constrained areas.

To sum up, a novel core–shell ratiometric fluorescent sensor, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP, has been triumphantly constructed for selectivity recognition and sensitive detection of trace OTC. Herein, an efficient sensing mechanism based on the synergistic effect of target-triggered IFE and AE enabled the sensing system to yield double and reverse FL signals, enhancing sensitivity and accuracy of the detection. Thanks to the modification of tailor-made imprinting sites, the established Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP demonstrated favorable selectivity and satisfying reusability. The good practical application of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was finally confirmed by monitoring the milk samples and water samples. Meanwhile, an instrument-free portable optical sensing platform by integrating Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP was also successfully developed for the onsite analysis of OTC. The homemade facile device was introduced as a darkroom containing the fixed excitation light source and sample cell, avoiding the interference from environment and improving the feasibility and accuracy of assay. The smartphone acted as a reader and analyzer achieved the visual sensing of OTC by converting the fluorescence color change into digital values, decreasing analysis time and cost. More significantly, no important difference was observed between smartphone-based sensing method and HPLC method during practical application. This work thus not only enriches the strategy for construction of the high-quality portable device, but also offers new insight for developing an efficient sensing platform for public health and environmental safety.
